entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, so now we're going to debrief TESO Women Development Initiative, which is UG021. Can you, Sharon, I guess start again with just a brief introduction to what this is about.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, so Tess, Women Development, that's tail day. In short, what they do is they have several activities about about six and they do recycling of agricultural waste. And they turn these into briquettes which they use as fuel for cooking, which is clean energy, easily available locally. And it's also much cheaper than the firewood which different people in Uganda have been using in the local areas. They also do capacity building for farmers. They have smallholder farmers whom they help in horticulture. They also help them with collective marketing for vegetables. And they also use irrigation. They developed some irrigation systems to help these farmers to be more resilient and improve their farm productivity and also help improve their household nutrition and agriculture incomes. And they actually use locally available materials to construct these irrigation systems so that in case a part on the irrigation system or the whole irrigation system breaks down, it's easy and cheap to replace them and repair them. And these irrigation systems are actually developed in collaboration with a local university here, with students from that local university. And they also help to do soil testing for farmers. And they also help in empowering women by teaching them how to save. They have some village saving skills, things that they do. And the other thing is helping them to learn how to make and also sell improved stoves, cooking stoves which use less fuel. So this also helps them to be able to cook and to at least have some more meals at the end of the day because of the very increasing fuel. The chapel this side is really expensive. So the improved stoves are helping with that. They also help to reduce deforestation through this. So in summary, what they're doing is recycling agricultural waste. They're also into livelihood and food security. They are helping with climate action and food resilience. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, how can you tell me a bit more about how they do this, about their programs? Like is it, I guess the clean cooking solutions is specifically those stoves, they're.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: They'Re.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Building them and supplying them or. Yeah, can you tell me a little bit more about how they do these programs?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, so when it comes to, to the improved cooking stoves, they make them themselves, they use, that's clay, that is soil. And then they build these, these cooking stoves which can cook a meal using very little, little firewood, but they don't even use firewood in these stoves. They use briquettes. And the briquettes the briquettes do not have smoke. It is clean energy. And they use their agricultural wastes. Let's say ginat G Net are they called pods. And they mix it with some other things and then they come up with. With briquettes. Okay, anyone. Yeah, anyone can easily use this because they usually have the things that they need for briquettes right there in their homes. So this is really good, seeing as the. The government even banned charcoal. So they also discourage people from cutting down trees and instead advocate that the people can use brickets instead as a better option, a cheaper option, and something which is readily available so they can still cook their meals, eat their food in a better and cheaper way.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. Okay, great. And can you tell me about the structure of the organization and how they. What they're. Yeah, if they have kind of donors or what their funding model is.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay. So TO is also an NGO and it's registered with. With. With a Uganda bureau. And they also have a board of directors. They have six employees currently. They said that before COVID they had 12 employees. But when Covid struck, they also lost some funding because of the COVID challenges. And currently they have. Some people have come in to fund this year's programs. I think it's for the next five years. So they have funding secured already for making brickets and stoves. But now they have been operating in about five different districts in Uganda. But they want to first concentrate in Soroti, just one district in Uganda this time so that they again build the work. And after that maybe they branch out to that district, different districts again. And let me see. So their business model is that. Okay, I think this started when Covid struck. The, When Covid struck, they. They really struggled with the funding because it was very minimal and they had to survive. So they started. They made more brickets, made more stores, and kept selling these. So the income which they got from selling the briquettes and the stoves really helped to sustain them, their families and even the organization. So currently they are selling. They have so many orders, even they sell. So Soroti is in eastern Uganda, but they even sell all the way to the central. To Kampala, our capital city. So they have different orders coming, coming in. And they have also now involved different women in their communities, people who are coming into their project to also help them sell these. These stoves and brickets. And then they, those people, the sellers are given. They're like commission agents. So they earn some commission from, from this business. And yeah, so that's what I got is that they use their stoves and brickets to get some money, but they also have funding and they also have, they have partnerships with different organizations who come in to help them and train some people in their communities in doing. Let me see, that was irrigation. There is also the brick. No brick. Wasn't there the brick and then there is the briquettes. So that's mostly it here. They have some donors, but they also try to do, to use their products to earn something from it.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, I guess this is leading into the stakeholders already. So I, you mentioned the partners who are helping them with training. Did they tell you any of the partners, who they are, what they're doing, like what is the relationship?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yes, they actually mentioned the people who gave them this, this current project's funding. Unfortunately, I did not write that down, but it's there in our audio. And they also mentioned that they have exchanges with MIT in Boston. That a group from MIT comes to their organization to Teodi, Uganda every January to teach young people innovation and entrepreneurship. And the target group is usually school going children who are in primary seven or between primary seven to senior four. So those are about, let me see, the age group is about 11 to 11 to 18. So yeah, so they come and teach those children and they also. Let me see the other stakeholders who they have. Yeah, the universities, they work with different universities because there is, there are some students who come from Busitema University to help them set up the irrigation system. So they have partnerships with universities. They also work with our local governments because they said that they are a small organization so it would be really expensive trying to hire more people. So they try to build relationships with the government and then as they're implementing some activities, they involve their local governments in these activities and then they work together. And also they have, Let me see, they mentioned women's savings group. Yeah, women's savings group. So they have different women in their communities whom they teach and they help them to manage their finances. They know how to save, how to maybe start a business, how to maintain that business. And also through these groups, the women get to access credit. Since it is very hard for women to access credit from commercial institutions seeing as they do not have collateral and yet most financial institutions ask for collateral. So these village savings groups help these women and they get to pull their money together. And at the end someone also gets to borrow from that very group. If she needs some money, she will be able to access that credit from within that group and she doesn't have to bring collateral because the Group itself has acts as collateral for the women. Yeah, and let me see. So there is. Yeah, I think that's mostly. So they also work with women farmers. They teach them good agronomic practices and they also give them irrigation systems. But these irrigation systems are actually not individually owned because they mention that once they bring the irrigation system, different farmers will have to share it in their different farms. So they have different people coming together to use these community irrigation schemes to help them keep growing their crops. And yeah, at the end, they are planting throughout. Their nutrition is improving. Their agricultural incomes are also improving. So it's really helping the different people and different women farmers in the communities.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, thank you. Who are they selling the brickets and the stoves to?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, so they sell to schools. Schools are now starting to see that it's better and cheaper to buy brickets instead of buying chapel charcoal and firewood. So, yeah, so they, they. They sell the brickets to different. To different schools and they also sell the brickets to supermarkets. But to the supermarkets, they package it in a different way. With schools, they also have some different. There's a different bag they use for schools and then for the supermarkets. She showed me. I think it's there also in the video clip. It looks a bit different and more corporate so that it can be displayed. It's cleaner. It looks clean as well. So they also have different people who keep ordering from them maybe to also do their own businesses. Yeah, so they have at least some good market out there. And the stores they, they sell to just different people in different communities who keep asking for the stoves because like individuals, individuals, those are community members, let's say. I also want some stoves in my house. Instead of using the ones I've been using, I will ask them to supply me with these ones.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. So they do have like a pretty stable business model where they're selling. Selling this and making income from a variety of different clients, I guess.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yes, yes. And they also have some stores. The first store is where they make the briquettes from. And then they take them to another one where the briquettes are dried. And then after, when they are ready for the market, they have a, A finer one, a bigger one where they store everything in and then it is dispatched. Taken to the market. So they've actually put in these. These. These different stores in place. They've built them there because the business is working for them. But unfortunately, when I went there, they things they haven't. Let me see, they're just again starting. They were planning to Start the production. So they had just a few leftover products which they showed me. But then they showed me these other rooms where they have been keeping the products, different packages, packaging. And yeah, so I believe that it's really working for them because they are trying to really commercialize it. It's helping them and the women in the community because the organization told itself earns from it. The commission agents also earned from the same. So it's really working.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. So I've added then to the stakeholders, the kind of the community members who are buying the stoves, the supermarkets as well, who are selling them, the schools were buying, buying them. All of that is, you know, people in their network that they're connecting with in order to do, in order to do their work. And then I'm thinking, is there anybody, anyone else maybe, But I think, I think that sounds. Oh, there's the staff, I was going to say probably right. Like if, if they have this storage unit, the stores place, place where it's dried, then they have a team of people kind of working to do all of this stuff.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yeah. And also the youth specifically because that when it comes to the irrigation system that the students in Bugema University come, come and they also, they also teach others. And some youth, I think some in Bugema University also come to learn water engineering because Todi also now has learned how to do that. So they also keep teaching them. So she mentioned that, that they work with the youth when it comes to the irrigation system and that they learn water engineering. Yeah, okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. Did, did she tell you about the challenges that they have to deal with?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yes, and the very first one still is to do with local government. Yeah, that. So the local government expects allowance, like sitting allowance, give them some money, some funding at each meeting. And yet their budget is limited. So they have had to sit down and tell them that no, we need to change the way we perceive things. This is for our own good. We should not be expecting to get an allowance for everything. And yeah, that. So she's really trying to tell them that. And slowly by slowly they are trying to navigate that. But she says, yeah, there is that challenge of what the local government expects. But at the end still they work together. She said so. And then she also talked about the unpredictability of funding that. Yeah, that they also have some challenges with that it's the funding is not readily available and that affects project activities. And that also the NGO bureau has some regulations which are too strict and which do not favor NGOs. Like they charge NGOs 2 million Ugandan shillings monthly. If the NGO has not, has not renewed their plan commit with the bureau. And yet, and yet the delays are usually with the government, the local government. The NGO will take their papers there for the local government to sign, but then the local government will take a really long time, even up to three months before signing on those papers. So that usually ends, that usually causes delays. But the NGO bureau does not look at that. All they want is to have the papers ready. And the, and the money they charge is really high for NGOs. So. Yeah, so that's also another challenge that she mentioned.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, that reminds me actually in the stakeholder mapping, because you mentioned the unpredictability of funding. Did they, did they say who their donors are or how? Yeah, how do they.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yes, she did, she mentioned. But I think I didn't write it down. But she talked about it during our, during our audio interview. She mentioned some organization. Let me see. I think I did not write their name down. Okay. From those people. Yeah. Okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: So I'll just. Yeah. Well, we can say that donors are definitely an important stakeholder because, you know, if they don't get the funding, then they can't do the activities. But it's okay if we don't know the exact donors.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: And then for the future, do they have any plans.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: For the future they want? Actually, she mentioned that she wants to ensure that she retires not very, maybe in five years from now that when she retires she, she wants to have someone, a successor so that the organization keeps going so that the activities of the organization do not stop and so that they keep on helping the community to improve. So she was, she said she's looking into how to make sure that the succession goes on, the succession plans are in place. And she's also, they also said that they are planning to reach more areas. It won't just be in eastern Uganda, but she hopes that they'll be able to reach out to the rest of Uganda. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay. And do you think it would be interesting to go back to this case and interview them again in two years?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Let me see. It would be interesting to know how the succession plans go and if the organization is still continuing and how also the irrigation schemes are working, that irrigation system, because usually I've seen that with communities, once an organization brings in something, let's say like an irrigation system, in case it breaks down, they don't want to repair it, they will usually say that belongs to so and so it belongs to this organization. Like they do not really own the thing, but she mentioned that they try to work with the communities so that they feel that this is their thing. So I think it's interesting to check back and see if the. The community is really involved fully in this thing and if it is going on without to involvement.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great. Thanks, Sharon. Anything else you want to share about this initiative?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: No, not at the moment. Not at the moment.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, 1, 2.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: And then we start.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: I'm Betty Ikalai. I'm the executive director of Tewidi Uganda. This is a women empowerment non government.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Organization which is registered with national NGO bureau. And we are based in Sorocha. As our headquarters, we work in five districts, that is Sorochi, Serere, Bukedia, Quen and Busia.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Our major activities run around different themes. One of them is we're looking at climate action and health.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So under this, we're looking at how.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Climate change is affecting the health of people in the community. And we have different activities that we do under that.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We are trying to see that we.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Promote deforestation in the community so as.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To recover the trees that have been cut down.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We also.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Train communities in making charcoal.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Briquettes as an alternative source of fuel for cooking instead of using firewood and wood charcoal, so that we reduce the amount of pressure that is put on.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Forests or on trees for fuel. And then we encourage use of saving cookstoves. We make the energy saving cookstoves through.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Our enterprise and then we sell to the communities. We make institutional stoves for institutions like schools, restaurants and hotels so that they.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Are able to at least use very little fuel.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And we customize them to using charcoal briquettes. So apart from that, we also.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, so 1, 2, we start.

entries.text: I'm Cole.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Betty Ekalan, I'm the executive director of TEWIDI Uganda. TEWDI stands for teso, Women Development Initiatives. It's an organization that was registered with National Bureau of Nano government organizations in 2014. And we do a lot of activities especially around eastern Uganda. Most of our activities concentrate around women empowerment and community development.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: One of the projects that we are.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Handling is working with women smallholder farmers to ensure that they can grow vegetables throughout the year. And in this way they are able.

entries.speaker: Betty

entries.speaker: Betty

entries.speaker: Betty

entries.speaker: Betty

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So we build their capacity on how.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To grow the vegetables, provide them with the resources like seeds and seedlings, and then also train them on post harvest.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Management as well as marketing of their.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Produce once they are ready for market.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So we've been working in Bukadia, Serere.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Sorochi, Quen and Busia.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And currently we're working in Soroti. We work in four sub counties, that.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Is Awukot Subcounty, Kamoda, Sam county, as Suret and Ochokchan. We've decided to concentrate on these areas because we want to see that our.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Impact is seen greatly within Soroti district where our headquarters is. Another project that we are doing is.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Imparting skills to the community, especially women and youth.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We believe that when somebody has some.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Skills, they are able to be self reliant.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So we train them especially on skills.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: For earning income, but also skills on.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: How to get alternative sources of fuel.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: For cooking instead of putting a lot of pressure on forests.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So we train communities to recycle agricultural.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Waste and city waste to be able.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To make charcoal briquettes so that they.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Can be able to use it for.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Cooking and then also train them on.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: How to train, I mean how to.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Plant more trees so as to.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Protect.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: The existing trees from being cut, but also to replace the ones that have been cut.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And most of the trees that we.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Encourage people to grow are fast growing trees so that they can supplement on.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: The fuel, but also fruit trees so.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: That they can have food for their households. Another important activity that we, we do apart from working with farmers and then also.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: I am Betty Ikalan, I am the executive director of TEWIDI Uganda.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: TEWIDI stands for TESO Women Development Initiatives.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Which is a non government organization registered with national NGO bureau. And we are based in Soroti City as our headquarters, but work in five different districts of Serere, Bukadia, Kwen, Busia and Ginger. So our main focus is women empowerment and community development. And we look at so much at.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Activities that aim at imparting skills to.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: The community so they can become self reliant. Our focus groups that we work with are women, women and young people, especially girls. We want to see that women become self reliant, become empowered to manage themselves and manage their families. Because we believe that unempowered woman has a very big impact she creates in her family. So that's why our entry point to the community is through the woman and the girl child.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: However, we make sure that you also work with the men because.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: You cannot.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Say you're not working with the men.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Because if you put the men aside.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Then your project can easily fail.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So that's why I was saying that we are an inclusive organization, but our major focus is the women and the girls.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So our projects are majorly.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We look.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: At livelihoods and food security.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Under this, we are working with women.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Smallholder farmers to ensure that we have.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Vegetables throughout the year. So the project that we have been.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Implementing under that is called dry season.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Horticulture for women smallholder farmers in eastern Uganda.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So in this project we ensure that.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: They get skills that can enable them.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To be able to produce vegetables throughout the year so that they can have.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Income and then also they have food for their families.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Another project that we have been doing.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Is imparting skills to the communities that we work in. And these skills involve helping them to see how they can earn income from the skills that they acquire.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And one of the major skills that we have been training the communities is.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: How to recycle waste to be able.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To get fuel for cooking. So this fuel is called charcoal briquettes.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And majorly they use the waste from.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Their gardens, from their households. And then also Teso region being one of the biggest producers of granites.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We're using the granite shells to make.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: The charcoal briquettes so that people can.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Be able to cook and reduce the pressure on the existing trees. In addition, we also try to encourage people to plant trees to replace the.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Trees that have been cut down for.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Charcoal and firewood, but also to ensure.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: That we try to mitigate other climate.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Change impacts that we are now feeling in the community of recent. The last past two months, I think.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Teso region experienced very extreme heat waves.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So people are really so stressed. Pregnant women suffered a lot because of that heat.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And then also we realized that the level of abortion for animals was high.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Because of the heat. So we're saying we need to see.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: What we can do to reduce this heat in our community. And that's why we're really pioneering the.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Project on tree planting.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And then we also look at how, apart from working with women farmers and then the young people, we want to see how we can be able to.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Train.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We build so much training other organizations.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: That are working with the farmers so.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: That they can know how to work.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: With these farmers especially. I learn to appreciate that these farmers.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Have a lot of knowledge that we.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Can be able to share and harness and be able to develop our communities.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So that is basically what we are.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Doing in our community. And we look forward.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We believe that STEMD Uganda has a brighter future.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And in a few years to come.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We will be able to reach out.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To more people than we are reaching out now.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And we should be able to train more people.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We work so much with different institutions.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Especially universities, because we believe that once they have that knowledge, to share that.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Knowledge in the community through giving opportunity.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: To students to do internships with us.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: And then through those internships, they are.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Able to touch the communities and also.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Share the knowledge with our team that is working with us in the office.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Thank you.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Thank you for giving me this time today to talk with you, to conduct this research with you. Would you please introduce yourself and your role in this organization and what the organization is, what it's about.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay. Thank you so much. I am called Betty Ekalan. I am the Executive Director of Tewd Uganda. TV Uganda stands for TESO Women Development Initiatives. It registered with the NGO Bureau Minister of Internal affairs as an indigenous non government organization. We began our activities in 2014 in the districts of Soroti. And then in 2016 we asked for them to give, we asked to scale to different districts. So from 2016 to 2019 we are working in Soroti, Bukadia, Serere, Quen, Ginger and then Busia. Then when Covid came, it somehow affected most of our activities and almost forced us to close because our donors were greatly affected by Covid and I think we even lost one of our major donors. One of the persons who was funding us died. She was an anonymous donor, but also others I think cut off. They had funding cuts so they could not continue. So from 2022 we didn't have funding. So we're surviving on income generating activities to be able to be able to continue our activities. So even we were forced to cut the number of staff that we had and also reduce on the activities. So currently after, as we recover from COVID the effects of COVID we've decided to first of all narrow to work in Soroti district as we see how we can scale up once we get more funding. From 2015 to 2019 we implemented a project called Innovations in Dry Season Horticulture in partnership with University of California Davis under the Feed the Children funding USAID program. But it ended in 2019. So currently we have a project that we are implementing in Soroti. It's called Farmer Led Nutrition Resilience Integrating Smallholder Horticulture, Soil Health and Economic Opportunities for Women Smallholder Farmers. So this one is going to be in Soroti district in four sub counties in eight parishes. So we want to do most of our concentration here until we are able to scale to other districts. The people whom we work with mostly are women and children. However, being a gender inclusive organization, we also believe that it's very important to include the men in everything that we do though our priority is the women and children, but you cannot do well with the men because you will not be able to succeed much in any community development work if you not involving the men. So we try as much as possible to ensure that everybody is involved. We work closely with the local government, local structures to ensure that the smooth running of the projects in the community and also this helps us to utilize the few resources we have well, instead of duplicating and duplicating some of the structures in the community in the name of having our own field staff. So we try as much as possible to include the local government and also for them to be able to know what we are doing and for us to be accountable to the local government that this is what we are doing. So we work hand in hand with them. Our themes, our areas of focus are mainly on livelihoods and food security. We are also looking at climate action and health resilience. It's one of the big issues now, in fact, in the current projects. That's what we're looking at. We want to see how has climate change affected the community, especially community health? What is its impact on the health of the community? So that's what we're trying to do at the moment. Then also we're looking at capacity building. We believe in imparting skills to young people so that they are able to forge their future. Can you close.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yeah, that's okay. So we're talking about capacity building mostly for young people.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: So we do projects here. We train young people especially on how to. How to recycle waste, how to recycle agricultural waste and city waste to make fuel, that is charcoal briquettes. So we make briquettes from here and we're able to sell to get some income to run the organization, but also impart skills to young people who also make improved cookstoves for households so that they can save on the amount of fuel that they have. Because it's a big issue now in the community. I think in the whole Uganda, it's difficult to get fuel for cooking. And you realize that 90% of the population in Uganda relies on charcoal and firewood. But because of the ban on burning of charcoal, it's caused a lot of commotion in the community. People need to cook, but charcoal is very expensive. So we're trying to look at skills that can help the community to have the resources that they need, especially fuel, but also impact skills for the young people. And then we also, we usually have a project that we do in partnership with mit, that is Massachusetts Institute of Technology, the lab in Boston. They come every January to work with our young people here. We call it Innovation and Entrepreneurship Youth Camp, whereby we try to give young people a chance to think outside the box and be able to think outside the box and be able to innovate, look at what are the problems in our community and what resources are there. How can they use those resources to solve these problems so that we're able to improve our community? Even if we don't have people from other places coming to help us, we should be able to help ourselves. So the young people from Boston come to Uganda and they work with our young people here. Usually we target from P7 to S4 because we believe they are still young and once they are trained, they can be able to change their mindset since they are still young. And we also look at them as our future leaders. So if you give them opportunity when they are still young, we could have a different set of young people who lead us, not waiting to be given jobs, but trying to see how they can create jobs for themselves. So that is one of the things that we do. And then we also work so much with farmers. We do capacity building of farmers and farmer organizations, I mean organization that support farmers. We train them especially on horticulture and then also collective marketing, because most times people grow their crops, but the middlemen come in and disorganize the farmers. They buy the things cheaply and then they go and make a bigger profit. So we're trying to train the farmers on how to produce quality crops, especially vegetables, but also be able to see how you can sell those vegetables and get more income other than relying on the middlemen. And majority of the farmers that work with are smallholder women farmers. Because we realize that most times the women are more disadvantaged. They cannot go very far to look for market for their produce. Sometimes they don't even have transport to help them. So we're trying to see how do we help them to be able to benefit from that. So that's why we've been having that project called Dry Season Horticulture for women smallholder farmers. We believe that vegetable growing is the easiest thing even a woman can do. She can do behind her kitchen and have a small income, even if she cannot get big, like large, large chunks of gardens for her to grow her crops. Because, for example, especially here in Teso, most of the land belongs to the man because of the patriarchal nature of our society. So it's the boys who inherit the land. It's the boys who inherit almost everything. So the girl child sometimes gets so disadvantaged. So even in terms of. Even when you get married, everything belongs to the man. When you're at home, everything is belonging to your father and your brother. So when you get married, everything is belonging to your husband. So you find that the women cannot. You can't even go and get a loan in the bank, because the bank will say, where is the collateral security? The plot belongs to the husband, everything, all the goats belong to the husband, the cows belong to the. Even you belong to the husband. So it disadvantages you so much. So we're trying to say, how do we empower women so that they can also have some little income that can support them, even if they are not able to really access bigger chunks of garden. But at least the little ones at home, they can be able to use that, then also support them if they are able to rent from other landlords and be able to scale their production. But for now, we look so much at what you can do at home, the kitchen, gardens, then you can be able to rent to scale your production. So that's what we do. And then also we do a lot of work around. Teaching these women to save. We believe that since it's very difficult to access credit in a bank because you don't have collateral security, once you're able to save, you will be able to plow that money to buy resources that you need in your small projects. And then also the group. If you belong to a group, the group first of all stands as a peer support system for you. But apart from being a peer support, it's also able to lend you money without needing collateral security because the group itself stands as a collateral security. So you don't need to again, ask your husband for this, ask your husband for that. And then also it gives you opportunity also to network and partner with other women and learn what is outside there. Because we find that there are some women, when you go to the community there, they have been born in Soroti, they studied here, they got married here, and they will die here. They never get a chance to move out. Even just across that Awaja bridge to outside the other side. They have never moved. They are just around here. But when you also get out of your home and come to a group, you get to hear from these other women what happens out there. And they encourage you, they encourage you. And then also they support you. So that's why we encourage them to be in village savings groups so that they can support each other and also empower themselves. So that's basically what I wanted to say about Tewd Uganda. Our work focuses so much around women empowerment, especially looking at the women's Mohelda farmers.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, wow, thank you so much for that. That, that's really clarifying and very clear. So would you also talk a bit about your legal structure?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay. We are legally registered with the national Bureau of Non Government Organizations. And you are sb Even with the district, we have an MOU that allows us to work in the specific sub counties that we're working in. And so I think we're legally registered. I think that is what we just wanted to say.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Because the basic ones you need is national, I mean NGO board or bureau. And then you need a ursb, you need to be registered. And then you have to also register the district where you work and make sure that even at the sub counties, you have to open a file and have an MOU and pay money for the certificate of registration at the sub counties to allow you to be to operate in those sub counties.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, wow, thank you. And how about the organizational structure of to Uganda? Yeah, so you have an executive director.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: You have the board, we have the board of directors, have the executive director. And then under the executive director, we have finance and administration. Under finance we have the accounts. Then we have the other support staff, like the driver, the office. Then now we have also the program officer. Under the program officer, we have the Community facilitators, those are the people who are based in the community. And of course the beneficiaries, the farmers. And then I also have the monitoring, though at the moment we only have the monitoring person. But also we're supposed to have other people working under the monitoring. But now we have monitoring. Then we have the implementation unit and then we have the finance. So that is our organogram. But the board oversees the work of the NGO and the executive director acts as conduit between the staff. Okay, that the secretariat and the board. And then also public relations with the donors and all that and reporting district, everything, networking. So you play a lot, a big role around that.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, and how big is the organization? Like how many people at the moment?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: After Covid, we had to downsize. Seriously. We used to be a team of 12 people, but now we've come back to a team of six people. But we know we're going to grow as projects come in, we shall be recruiting more people. But for the projects that are at the moment we have six people. That's the executive director, then we have the programs officer, and then we have the finance and administration manager. Then we have the accounts and then we have the driver and we have office messenger. And then now we are using the local government structures at the community, but we are going to recruit community based facilitators at the community to help the program officer mobilization and all that.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, and your relationships with these people, are they like family, friends, employees or. Because at least you have mentioned that there are some. Okay, work with the local government. And then yes, you have the driver, you have the office messenger, you have the programs director. Like what's your relationship with them?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: We have to, we have to, according to our policy, have to recruit staff. You don't bring in your relatives, you don't bring in your children or at all. This is a national organization and it has to follow the art, follow the policies that are existing. So we advertise, we recruit people and then we are able to help them. However, like for the young people, like those ones, sometimes we outsource them. When like maybe we have work, we outsource them and train them or maybe tell them come and do this casual work. We have also those ones. But for the people on the payroll, they have to be recruited and they have to pass through interviews and then if they, they pass through, then they are taken in. Okay, so.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Would you also share maybe some noteworthy achievements or milestones which you have reached as an organization? Because you talked about there is a time the organization went through a tough strain when the funders pulled out a bit. But then you had to rely on your skills. That's really wonderful. So would you maybe tell us more about how any achievements which you have heard and some testimonies from your work?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah, I think one, first of all, we have been able to make partnerships with prestigious organizations. Like we're working hand in hand with mit. I think you know, MIT is one of the most prestigious universities, Boston. So work with the Adilab department to train students from MIT that come to Uganda. We train them, they come and do the internship here, work with us and then they go back and get their Masters. And then also work with university. In fact we have, we do a lot of work with universities. So work with mit, we work with University of California Davis and then also University of California San Diego for the current project. And then now in Uganda, we also partner so much with Bustema University because we had a project that was looking at irrigation, small scale irrigation. So we needed to have universities coming to support us and work together with them. So the fact that we are able to attract these prestigious universities to work with us already shows that they have, they have what trust in our organization and in what we can do. And then two, we have also worked, we work with farmers and maybe before we enrich farmers, local government, we have a very good relationship with them. So most times you find that when you need any support, they are always there standing with us, they are able to help us. So that is one of the things that we would be proud of. And we've supported many groups. We are working on that project funded by usaid. It was a very big project. It was around, I think we received about 1 billion IFTC Uganda shillings. It was about US$350,000 in partnership with University of California, Davis. But the funding was from usaid. And we did a lot of work in five districts. That was Sererek, Bukedia, Quen, Busia and Ginger. And there we did different innovations. If you go to our office, you look at the different innovations on the wall. That is what we did. We did a lot of work around that. And up to today everybody remembers this project. And we also had opportunity to train almost 52 students from Bustema University. Some of them are now holding big positions in government because of the training that we gave them to work, especially students who are doing water engineering because we gave them opportunity to work with the farmers, support the smallholder farmers in putting small applications of irrigation and the small innovations that they could do to produce the vegetables. And then the farmers were able to produce large scale of vegetables and be able to map markets around their production sites and be able to sell. The team in Busia was even cruising with the vegetables to Kenya, the other side of Kenya. They would take the cowpeace leaves, they would take the sukuma, they take all the things. And I think because of the mobilization that we did, the training that really helped us. But one important thing that I think would really be so proud of is also involving women in decision making. Decision making on issues concerning irrigation. Most times anything that concerns a machine is that's the domain of a man. Women, you don't even say anything. You just watch when they are touching machines. But in our project, we made sure that women were. We had 50, 50, 50, 50 representation in decision making, executive committees. So women were represented, women were able to start the machine, women were able to touch machines. They really understood what their project was all about. And therefore they are able to decide whether they want to be part of it or not, whether they are able to negotiate with the landlords to rent for them land so that they scale up their production. I think in Bukharia we had challenges whereby the landlord rents out land to the women. And after the women have cleared for one year and they have started growing their crops, when he sees the gardens have been cleared and they are good, he comes and wants to take away the land from them and rent it to the men who are growing rice. So the women said, but we put in a lot of money to clear this. We are not going to accept that. We have to sign an agreement. Give us like two years to, first of all, till this land, grow our vegetables. And then after two years, if you want to take back the land, you can take it, if not, you can renew. So they are able to bargain and even go to the LC and the subcountry and caused the landlord to act as would be better, other than him just pulling away the land just because the land is his. And yet they have used a lot of money. So I felt that was empowering the women, they did not wait for us to go and bargain for them, but they took the thing to themselves and looked for the right legal procedures and had amicable communication. I mean, discussion with the landlord came out with an agreement. And when we went, they told us, this is what we did, and this is the agreement we got. And this is our gardens and we're going to produce our vegetables at least for two years before we can hand over the gardens back to the landlord when our period has expired. So I think that was really something interesting. And then also in some sites, we could see women able to hold these sprinklers and be able to. To go and irrigate their gardens without having to wait for the man. Most times you'll find the men were the ones to start the engine. You are the ones, and they put you on ransom. If they don't want to start the engines. Women cannot do. And most times they take the things and irrigate their own gardens. They don't give women a chance, but women got a situation where they learned how to irrigate for themselves. And for me, I felt that was part of empowerment. And then now, when you come to other projects, like I talked about, fuel for cooking, we know that even if there is food and there is no fuel for cooking, it's difficult to eat that food. So we have that component whereby we also encourage the women that, okay, even if the vegetables are there, we also want us to get a skill where we can be able to make charcoal briquettes, sell those briquettes, get some income, but also have briquettes, I mean, fuel for cooking at home. So these women are able to recycle the waste from their gardens and be able to make fuel and they can have food instead of cutting trees. So at least what we're encouraging is have three meals a day for the children so as to avoid malnourish, malnourished children. So if you can be able to provide for your children. And most times they told us that the reason why you cannot cook three meals is that there's no charcoal. So, so sometimes you cook one sauce, and then you keep on serving that sauce for lunch and supper, because you can't cook a different sauce for supper since you don't have charcoal or firewood. And in the village now, even firewood, people are buying it. Before, you used to just go to the garden and you pick anybody's garden, but now you can't even just go and cut anybody's tree just like that. So people are buying firewood, and you find that you're buying maybe 5,000, a small, maybe five pieces of firewood, which cannot cook for you the whole day. So we are really trying to say, let's look for alternatives for cooking, alternative fuels. Then also in our projects, in fact, in the present project, we're looking so much at how do we do reforestation. So as we're cutting down the trees for charcoal, can we replant these trees as well? Saying that let's stop using the charcoal. Can we give people alternatives? So those are the things that Uganda is looking at in the community and in its activities with the community.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, wow. Thank you so much. And so what are the major expenses which are incurred by your organization as you run the activities?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay. Of course the first one is staff costs. You need to maintain the team that is doing all these activities. So we have the staff costs, maybe salaries, wages for those that are doing casual work. And then also we have operational costs to run the office. We need money for maybe like board meetings, you need money auditing, like maybe hiring people do audit audit reports and then say a few things that you need to run the office, stationary and all that, utilities like water and electricity. And then also for implementing activities, we need money for program activities going to the field. We need fuel, you need. Okay. Like transport. We need to have transport go to the field and all that. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay. And you said that the price primary funders.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: This year we have one organization, it's called Foundation S. They are based in France and they are funding one one of the projects, but others are on the pipeline. But the one that is being funded now is from Foundation S? Yes.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay. And now your financial sustainability, how would you assess that like under the current and future conditions?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay. Before COVID came, most of the time we are relying on donors because we had money donors. But after Covid we learned so many lessons. So we're thinking that for us to be sustainable, we also need to do other income generating activities that can help generate some income for the organization. For rainy days when things things are not good. In case another Covid came, we should not close, but we should continue surviving and serving the community. So apart from money we get from donors, we also look at other income generating activities. It's a project. I'll show you some of the things that we are doing here, especially making cookstoves and then also charco briquettes. This help us a lot in generating some income. Also want maybe do other projects like mushroom growing and any other thing that we can do and be able to sell and generate some money to supplement on the money that you get from funders. Because also realize now the funders the amount of money they give people is getting less because of especially I think apart from COVID the coming of Trump and his, I don't know, his declarations, we don't even understand what is happening is affecting people so much. Especially is I think the pronouncements on usaid. So we just feel that we are not safe because many donors are going to maybe follow suit and start cutting their funding. And so we need to look at which other ways can we be able to survive. And then also two, we are trying to train a community that is not dependent so much on money. We are trying to say that, okay, even if we have very little for just the trainers to come and train you, but can we be able to do some things without money? Because we are giving you knowledge that is already something. So can you be able to work on that? You want to empower people so that they can be able to do their own things without having to wait for somebody to help them then also we're trying to see how we can negotiate so much with the district and the local government to ensure that not every meeting that we have we should pay, not everything that we need should pay. So we're trying to, as much as possible for them to own these projects because we're also supporting them or supplementing local government activities by being in their sub counties and implementing these projects. So that is one of the fights we're having with the local government team. They have been used to, whenever you go for a meeting, you have to sign money, you have to do this. But we are saying that times have changed and we don't have that money. But we need to work with you. So please, can you understand and not make life difficult for us? Yeah. So I think that's what we want. But I believe that with the new strategies that we have, we should be able to survive even if another Covid came back. Compared to last time, I think we had a very big comfort zone. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Calls everyone.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: So what actually motivates you to do what you do and what are your values? What is your purpose?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay, first of all, this organization, maybe I didn't tell you the genesis of TEW with Uganda, Tewd Uganda was started by me and other women, women who saw the need to empower women, especially women and girls, women and children, to ensure that at least they have some skills that can enable them to engage in some economic opportunities to support themselves. And the reason is because they are increasing levels of poverty, especially in Teso region. And then also cases of gender based violence due to poverty. So you find that there's a lot of domestic violence in families and the reason is that most of the women are relying so much on the men for everything and the men sometimes get fatigued. So it leads to a lot of domestic violence. So we said, can we start up an organization that is focusing so much on women and Young people, the women, is that we want to empower them with skills so that they can also be self reliant. And then for the young people, we need to change their mindset to stop thinking about looking for jobs, but learn how to create jobs and then also impart them with skills through capacity building so that as they grow up, they have skills that they can survive on. So that's why we started this organization. Everything is just around empowering communities, especially women. And that is what motivates us. And then maybe I can also add a small thing. When I went to do my masters in the Netherlands, before I went there, I had 12 years of work in Teso region. I was working with an organization, working with people living with HIV aids. And most of my work was around in the communities I worked. I was in charge of Amuri, Akatakwika, Vramaido and Soroti. So I had opportunity to, to move in the community and I would really see the problems people are going through, especially women. So when I went for my masters, I said I need to do something that can help pull these women out of this. How can I be able to make an impact in the community? So I came up with these other women who had like minded thinking like me. And we decided that, okay, can we work together and see how we can help one another? So when I was there, one of the things that really touched me so much while I was doing my work in the community was fuel. You find these women living with HIV AIDS up those days. It was difficult even to get treatment. It's not like now where somebody works with HIV AIDS like it's something normal. Those days you'd see this malnourished woman on the veranda. Everybody is seeing, everybody's accusing you like it's you who brought the hiv. They don't even think that the man could have also been the one who brought the hiv. So it's you who is aware everybody's stigmatizing you and sometimes you don't have energy. You can't even go and look for this firewood. And I was saying if we had another source of another source of fuel that could make work easier for these women, that would be the best thing that we can do. So that's how Gouven came out with that project on charcoal briquettes, making charcoal briquettes. So we started with that project as just one of the projects whereby we put women in groups to train them how to make women briquettes with their hands. But later we realized that we could Even sell this and make it into a business that can generate income. So we went ahead and got manual machines. But still the briquettes were not strong enough like we wanted. So we went ahead and got motorized machines and we realized that people were buying abricates. So we decided to register it as an enterprise of the organization. And it's generating money for the organization. So now it's a fully registered enterprise. But we are able to sell. We sell to schools. People are using abricates in schools, they are using in orphanages. There is a big orphanage here called Avmechet. So most of the children, orphans are taken there and they use abricates for cooking. Some big hotels like Ineku Village are using abricates. Households around here are using and also now trying to see how can you train more women to sell these brickets on commission. So they also get money for themselves, but give us also backs the money that we need for the what to run the organization. So I think that is how it all came about.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Wow, that's really wonderful how everyone gets to benefit out of it.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah. Not just one person. Wow.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: And like your purpose, has it maybe evolved? Has it changed a bit since you started? Like over time, have there been any changes? Maybe. Have you. Maybe you started a certain way, but now you're doing things in a different way?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah, I think there have been changes. We have grown. When we started, we're just looking at just this small space, I mean like Soroti district. But then before COVID came, we were able to enlarge to four more districts, in fact five more districts, and were able to reach out to a larger number. And then also the projects that we started, we started them small with just a thinking of if you can do this just as a group of women. But now we realize that we're able to reach out to many people using our products, the stoves and the briquettes. So it's grown. It's just not because we started making just Lorena stoves in people's kitchens, but now we're making commercial stoves. We make stoves in schools. They are called institutional stoves. And then also we make household stoves that people are able to buy. And we're catering for everybody. These stoves can. They are those ones for small size, which school students, like students from ucc, from university can buy border borders who are single can use. Then we have those ones that are targeting households, we have those bigger ones that are targeting restaurants. So we're trying to touch everybody. And then also in Terms of the organization itself, we have grown. We started from my backyard, in my home. I just put a small room there. But now we have a bigger place here and we hope that maybe with time we shall be able to build a good office and then you can. For now, we started with this, with this container, our office. But we have a very big dream. We already have like a plan which is already approved by the city for us to build a storied building for our offices. But we decided that let's start with humbly, what matters is that we are reaching out to the people is not about the building. You're seated. It's not about this grasshopper seated. But it's about what you're able to do in the community. Instead of spending all the money on buildings, can we use that money to reach out to the community? So we believe that we're growing and we believe that we are going to grow bigger than this if we can just be able to stabilize and make a strategic plan to look further than just the donors.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: And are there any key challenges which you have faced maybe throughout your organization's history and maybe strategies which you implemented to resolve those challenges?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah, I think we've had challenges. First of all, we shall start even from the name of the organization. We started this organization in 2009 as a CBO and that CBO was called TESO Women Development Initiatives. Then we just put within word. But then in 2014 when we went to register with NGO board, we didn't know that you needed to reserve this name earlier. So when we went to register, we found another organization had almost a similar name to ours. So it affected us. We already had. They had TESO Women Development Association. Ours was TESOME and Development Initiatives. So URSB refused us to reserve the name. So we're saying what we do now. We already have a website, we already have money in the bank. Already having. Everything is. Everything is running smoothly. The donors know us at state of mind. So we had to work backwards and talk to URSB and say now what do we do? So they told us, okay, you can just change briefly. Maybe just put Tawidi, then add Uganda in front so that you remain in the same. What? But so that we are not clashing with the other organization. So that's how we didn't. We had ignorance about these registrations. We thought as long as you registered as a cbo, everybody knew your name in the whole country. We didn't know. We just known within our district only. So by the time we went there, we are Just seeing stars. But we've worked that out. And now secondly, another challenge we had was, Of course, the COVID was a big challenge. It brought so many issues and even led to downsizing of the staff. And then sometimes you can also have staff turnover as another challenge, especially when you don't have enough funds to pay people. You train them and then after you've trained, then again a person leaves and goes to another place, you have to train again. So it affects. So we're trying to see that as we grow, we also want to grow with our staff. How do we motivate them apart from money, so that they look at this as their home, their little home that they would want to spend some time before. But of course, we don't stop people from going to other places if they want to go to those places. But we are also trying to say that, okay, even if they went to other places, at least let them stay some time here so that you can benefit from the training that we gave to them. So basically it's just been about funding the effects of COVID and then maybe registration issues. And then recently, whenever you want to renew the permit of the organization, things have become so hard. So you find that getting the. There are so many documents that they want, and those documents from the district sometimes becomes very hard for you to get because there's need for money every corner. So you find that it makes it difficult for you to really get the document so fast. And then I think NGO Bureau has also put in that they charge you penalty of 2 million if you delay to renew your permit. And yet the problem is within here, you're trying to move, but you're not getting all the documents that you need quickly. So to help you to beat the deadline for NGO Bureau. So we're saying if you're charging us 2 million per month now, if these people delay with our papers like three months, we're paying 6 million. It is beyond us. We can't push the car, we can't push the LCA5 that sign the papers and all that. So sometimes it's quite hard for us, but we are surviving and we know we will survive.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: And so to run your organization, so successfully managing it like which critical knowledge and skills are required.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah, one of them is leadership skills. We've had opportunity to attend different trainings on leadership, especially a leader, women leaders, you know, there are so many challenges we face as women leaders. First of all, a leader in the organization, your mother told me, a wife at where are you all that? So there's a lot on your word. So for you to be able to oversee and run the organization well, you need those skills on how to be able to run. I mean, how to be able to multitask and, and yet be able to still work hand in hand with everybody. And also sometimes if you're leading more men, the men feel they should be the ones to lead, not a woman to lead them. But as long as you know what you're doing, you're able to do that. So leadership skills is one of them and then two. We also needed training. Like for example, for me, I'm a social worker by profession. My first degree is in social work and my second degree is in gender and women's studies. But I also needed skills in financial management for non finance managers. You need to also have some skills on how to manage finances so that it's not only the accountant who knows how what is supposed to be done and all that. You also have to do that. So you need some financial skills. You also need skills on how to network with other people. And then of course, public relations with the public relations and fundraising resource mobilization. So you really need those skills in order for you to raise funds from maybe donors and all that and maybe take advantage of opportunities that are within the districts for you to be able to lead your organization well.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Okay, so what are your key strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay, I could say first of all my. Maybe the strength. I don't know, you'll see where to place them because the SWOT analysis sometimes disturbs me. But I'll try to say one of the things is that one of the strengths that I have is being able to negotiate. So sometimes you may be working with a board if you're not very good at negotiating and being clear with what you're doing. Sometimes some board members can over they overstep where their limits are. So you need to be able to negotiate with them. You need to be very clear, demarcating boundaries on what is happening. That's why I think I have some negotiation skills. And then also, And then also facilitation skills. So I'm, I'm a trainer. I can't train people in the community. Maybe weaknesses. Sometimes I'm a perfectionist. I want to do everything. So I get broken down a bit like I want to do this. I feel like when you came, I was working on the letters to go to the community. I know that should be the work of program officer, but I always feel like things should be there. I need to read through and make sure there Are no mistakes. I don't want spelling word and all that. So that is my weakness. I just want to see things done very well. And so that. Because that's a representation of the organization. So if you leave things carelessly to go out, then somebody will read the letter and say what is what type of organization that cannot even write a letter. So I always feel that I should check on this. I should check on this. And then even in other activities, I feel like I should also look at and make sure that everything is running as smoothly. But I'm also able to delegate. I'm also able to delegate, but I delegate while wanting to see the final product. Because you. You may delegate and then at the end you decide what you're disappointed with some results. So make sure. I make sure that after I've delegated, once I've done the work, I want to see what has been done. Even finances. I want to see what has been done. I want those reports and all. And I want reports on time. And sometimes maybe some people are not able to give the reports on time, so I really need to push them and all that. So maybe they think, hey, you are so, so hard on us. But I just want everybody to. To be able to work at a good pace. So I don't know. You'll reorganize my skills.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: That is very clear. Yeah, that is very clear. And which advice would you give to others within the community based on your own experiences, to anyone out there?

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: For the organizations that are working in the community, The. The advice I would give them is to. To know that there's. The communities have some indigenous knowledge, so we need to respect them first.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Sorry.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Okay. Maybe faster.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: I think I'd finished for other people. And then also I wanted to advise like other organizations that are working with the local government. That is very important to work hand in hand with the local government and also ensure that you respect people in their different offices. Sometimes you find that because we're in the. Because we're in the. In these offices you have the man. We just rush to the field without reporting to somebody's office to tell them what you're doing. And the subcounty, you go and do your activities in the field. But if you get any problem within the community, it is that the subcounty and those other local structures to protect you. So I would advise that when you work with the community, make sure you hand in hand with the local government structures. The parish chiefs, the subcounties chiefs, the committee officer, the health assistants, the sakaos, all those people are really helpful for you. Even at the district courtesy call to the district so that they can know you. Because when your funders come, or even anyone who wants to know about you, they could even decide just to go there and they can be able to get information about you. But if you want to work alone, it becomes very difficult. So that is one thing, that one then also include being inclusive when you're in the community, include everybody not only working with particular people. Sometimes some people want to work with the men on, leave out the women. But it's very important because women, women are part of that community. And so we need to ensure that we listen to their voices and empower them also to be able to maybe survive in the community. And then for the. For the community people. For me, I think my advice would be that everything is not about money. So sometimes when you call for a meeting, you should not all the time expect money to be given to you, expect money to be given to you. Sometimes you can work with that money. So what for us we do now as an organization, most of the committee meetings, we have them in the afternoon when people have already had their meal at home, so that we are not spending a lot in such a way we are able to sustain the activities of the organization. But if you go there and you have to cook food for people, you have to give people lunch allowance transport refund is very difficult. And then two, we try as much as possible to take the projects within the villages so that people don't walk long distance to come for meetings to come and do these activities. So I think that is also what others can emulate. So I think basically that's my advice. And then maybe also other people, maybe I can give advice maybe for you people who are doing research. Most times we give out our data, a lot of data. And then sometimes maybe we want the data to be respected. And then also maybe it is going to be used. For example, Nava at least saying the consent. But other people come and what interview you and then you'll see your information on the what and then you don't know. I didn't give you permission to put out everything. I had an interview with the team from Monitor newspaper. We had a very good interview here. But when it went there, they changed everything and just wrote what would sell their newspaper. And then I saw a very big article about my organization in the newspaper, about the enterprise in the newspaper. But it was not what we discussed here. So when I called the person who came to interview me, I said, I think that was my auditor who. But I said, but it was my information. Why didn't you get back to me and say, please confirm this before you just put it up there? So even when you to share the information, it's just good to confirm the person that now we're going to publish this. I hope this is a representation of what you said. So that is basically what I wanted to share.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Thank you. So what are your future plans like, where would you like to go and what do you want to avoid? What's your vision for the future of the organization?

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: I think our vision is to see that we reach out to many people, let many people benefit from the organization. But we also want to see that we don't run thin wanting to be everywhere because you want to reach out to very many people and yet your impact is not being felt because you've made yourself very thin. So we are trying as much as possible to make sure that we work. That's why even from the five districts that we have been working in, we've decided to come back first for this project. We're going to work in Soroti only. And then in Soroti we'll have four parishes, I mean four sub counties and in eight parishes. We just want to concentrate around here and make sure that our impact is felt before we can move into more districts so that we are not overstretched. And also the staff is not overstretched. And then our impact is not really felt in the community. And then two we also look forward to since now we are growing to mentor young people who will come and take over. Because I'm not going to die with the organization. I would not want to leave that Lagos where they say she died and the organization died. So I would want to see that it continues to support the target group. So I'm mentoring young people who should be able to do these activities. And then maybe with time, I just look at myself. Maybe in five years time, I just move to the board and sit at the board just to oversee what they are doing, but admire what they are doing because I have mentored them. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Wow, that's really sweet. Just five years. Wow.

entries.speaker: Sharon
entries.text: Yeah. I think in five years I should be retiring, but I'll just sit in the boat and do my personal things. Yeah. Because I really feel that you need to retire early so that you're so raised early.

entries.speaker: Betty
entries.text: Yeah, that's really retiring. Ali. Well, thank you so much for your time. And I think I will now first pause this.
